Alright, your back, let's rock and roll before we get to the good stuff, which is referrals how to build a massive stream of them, right? Before we get there, I want to do a little bit of housekeeping and sum up what this module has really been about in terms of protecting these new relationships that you've gotten as a result of the straight line. Remember, it costs a heck of a lot more money to get a new customer into service in an existing one. So when you actually do get a new one, you want to protect that relationship and make sure that it grows. In fact, I said last time, I think it was the last lesson, depending on the industry, the cost of revenue from a first-time client is typically 6 to 700% higher than from an existing one. So given that after expenses, typical business drops maybe 15 to 30% to the bottom line, your ability to make the most out of each new client is going to be crucial to the long-term success of both you and your company. You get it? So with that in mind, here are six strategies that will help you essentially magnify your sales roles by taking those customers that you open and ensuring that you make the most of them. Now again, some of you have been through already, so it's kind of a refresher here before we get into referrals here. So number one, which I started off with in this module was don't duck phone calls. And of course, I said this before, right? But I just want to reiterate right now how absolutely toxic this is to a long-term relationship. Again, once you duck a phone call, your relationship will never really be the same. You can repair it, you can mend it so to speak, but there's some element of that absolute trust, that blind trust that just gets lost. And the crazy part is, I remember I said earlier that people will accept that things go wrong. They'll accept bad news. If you tell them, in other words, don't just hide behind your phone or a text, you'll answer people or even better yet, reach out to them proactively to know there's a problem instead of them having to call you and then you're saying, up, tell you, I'm not here. Once you do that, the relationship is really never the same. So please, please, please do not duck phone calls. Give the customer the respect to tell them bad news yourself best proactively. That worst, when they do call, make sure you pick up that phone and you reset those expectations. All right? Now number two, I want you to consider sending your customers to your competitor. Now let me tell you what I mean by that. You have to be careful here, but if you can actually help a customer and you have a competitor who can, then you shouldn't be completely against sending them to your competitor. If you do that, you develop such massive goodwill and it'll be the start of them viewing you as a source or resource for them to get whatever they want, whether they think you might have it or not. In other words, maybe they say, well, you probably don't have the product I need, but if I go to them, they'll just tell me where to get it. You become this stop, that first stop for people when they're shopping. That's a really powerful way to develop a long term relation for the client. Like the movie, a miracle on 34th Street, right? The Macy's Santa Claus, of course, happens to be the real Santa Claus, right? He sends one of the store's customers, the mother who's trying to buy ice skates for her daughter, right? He sends her over to Gimbles, which is Macy's number one competitor back in the days, right? Because Macy's doesn't carry their type of ice skate, right? Now of course, the executives at Macy's are absolutely furious at first because their Santa is sending customers to their direct competitor. But as the story of Macy's good will starts to spread like wildfire in terms of how they put their customers' needs above their own desire to turn a profit, they end up generating so much publicity for free that it far outweighs the value of a pair of ice skates they could have sold. You get it? Now, obviously you've got to be very careful with this strategy, but it's definitely something to consider here depending on the situation. Let me give you another example that just happened to me this last week. My dad passed away, right? And here's the deal. I go to get a black suit at Breone, top maker of men's suits, custom made suit, $8,000, $9,000. And I walk in there, actually called the head, they knew I was coming, right? They'd open the store early for me. I walk in there and after I try on a couple of suits, it quickly becomes apparent they don't have the right type of suit for me. I'm not easy to fit off the rack. They have to custom make it. They know I have an event coming up, a funeral, right? In the next few days. And they're like, oh, you know, why don't you go to Tom Ford? I was like, I was so blown away that goes, go see Tom Ford. They have a lot of black suits. Or is there direct competitor Tom Ford for men's suits? All right? He sends me to Tom Ford and goes, and boy, they don't have it. Call me back and I'll send you somewhere else. I just got to tell you that I was with my son, Bowen Carter, and I walked out and I said, did you guys see what just happened to him? I forever feel indebted to Breone right now. Like they just did something that, you know, ingratiated themselves to me. I'm like, you know, whenever I want something, I'm going to go to Breone right now because that's the sort of level of caring and customer service that I value. So don't consider this to be just a stupid notion. Like, oh, I'd never send it to my competitor now. Obviously, if you're in a business where, let's say you're a landscaper, you know, and you get a sent to your competitor and you lose the account and it's only one narrow product, maybe that might not be a very small thing to do. Okay? And there's other businesses that just like that. But in many, many businesses, if you sell lots of different items and you don't have the particular one that's the best fit, then you go do that to your customer and you watch what happens. You will truly have a custom for life that will start looking at you as that first place to go to that go to store, that go to company for whatever their needs are, even if they're not quite sure that you'll have it in stock for them because they know that if you don't, you'll send them to the right place. That's good business. All right? Now, number three, and I already mentioned this to you when I talked about Michael Ovid's the super ace from Hollywood, right? I want you to write effective thank you notes. But I want to discuss exactly what kind of language you use and get specific here. Now, number one, your notes cannot be dry or boring and they certainly cannot come from a purely logical perspective. For example, let's say someone gave you a pair of candlesticks as a gift, just making that up, right? You wouldn't want to write them a thank you note and say, dear John, thanks for the candlesticks. Much appreciated. Best, Jordan. You get it? It's not going to go a long way with them. Rather, you write something along the lines of, dear John, thank you so much for those gorgeous candlesticks. My wife absolutely adored them. We keep them right on our dining room table and people are always commenting about how beautiful they are. They really warm up our room. Sincerely, Jordan. Now that's an example, but notice just how I use the emotional perspective, not a dry logic one. So just make sure when you're writing your thank you notes, use words that evoke an emotional response. Words like, I loved, I adored, I genuinely liked. You get it? You want to use words that go beyond the logical mind and tug on a person's heartstrings. Just trust me. If you go that extra mile here, that extra inch actually, really, to take time to construct an emotionally reactive thank you note, meaning it gets an emotional response from the person reading it. It will go a really long way to essentially anchoring that person into you emotionally as someone who cares, which is the basis of all rapport. It's your second 10. You get it? And it sets the stage for a long-term, mutually beneficial relationship. All right? And with that, I want to stop for a moment here. Let's take a quiz. Also there's some really important exercises to go through. So go there right now. And if you can, hurry back. Let's continue on with the next lesson as soon as possible. I'll see you there.
